author: soispoke
content: I think we should keep the bitlist. The main benefit is that it lets proposers avoid selecting censoring payloads at selection time, without it the proposer is blind to IL satisfaction and the builder is only punished after the fact, which is actually a liveness issue: you have more chances of selecting a payload than then gets reorged without the bitlist because you’re « flying blind »

author: soispoke
content: I agree the mechanism and the CR works without it

author: soispoke
content: But in this case it’s about liveness not CR

author: jih2nn
content: Assuming it's as a trustless payment, what is the reason for a builder to produce a censoring payload?

author: jih2nn
content: Even if the bitlist is good, the payload can be censoring one.

author: jih2nn
content: It's a "signal" and all payloads are expected to be IL-compliant by default.

author: soispoke
content: The idea is that it’s not rational for builders to lie

author: soispoke
content: But if they are not you want to skip them right away not wait until the payload is reorged

author: potuz
content: In addition to Thomas's point. There's also the benefit that it allows the proposer/P2P to choose/propagate  bids that are compatible with their forkchoice view. Without this, a builder that hasn't seen the right includers may even prevent other bids from propagating over P2P, so the right bids don't even reach the proposer

author: jih2nn
content: How the right bids fail to reach to the proposer?

author: potuz
content: They paid less therefore they're dropped

author: soispoke
content: Yeah the bid value is the only signal available without the bitlist

author: jih2nn
content: Such builder can just produce a good bitlist.

author: soispoke
content: It’s not rational for them to do that though

author: marchhill
content: as some of you know, yesterday steel

author: jih2nn
content: I don't see why a censoring builder behaves honestly only for the bitlist.

author: potuz
content: Yes but there's a difference on doing this from malicious behavior than just honest but unfortunate one

author: jih2nn
content: It's irrational to produce a censoring payload either.

author: potuz
content: The builder may not have seen the includers

author: potuz
content: This also protects such builders from having to pay for a Payload that will be reorged

author: soispoke
content: Yeah it means the builder is able to choose bids based on its current view

author: soispoke
content: of the ILs it’s seen

author: jih2nn
content: A proposer can choose the highest bid anyway. It doesn't protect the builder.

author: potuz
content: That's again not good behavior and on top of this if the proposer chooses the wrong bid and the bit list is included then attesters will reorg it's block

author: potuz
content: So he may as well not propose

author: jih2nn
content: No, it's not. The bitlist is not part of forkchoice.

author: potuz
content: It should be

author: potuz
content: You should not vote for a block that promises not to satisfy your view of IL

author: jih2nn
content: A payload with a subset bitlist can be IL-compliant. What matters is payload, not the bitlist.

author: soispoke
content: The rule should be the subset one

author: soispoke
content: As in it’s only reorged if the payload includes less txns than the ones it committed to via the bitlist

author: potuz
content: No, the bit list should be not of which txs were included, but of which includers are satisfied. Eg: if my Payload is full and I haven't included a single txs from includers I need to set a full bit list anyway

author: potuz
content: If it's not like this then the whole design is wrong

author: jih2nn
content: No, it happens when ILs overlap.

author: soispoke
content: That’s the design, bitlist of ILs not txns

author: potuz
content: Then an attester should know that a Payload is compatible with their view just knowing the bitlist

author: soispoke
content: at least here https://ethereum-magicians.org/t/epbs-focil-compatibility/24777

author: potuz
content: And I don't get how a subset is compliant

author: jih2nn
content: It's when a missing IL doesn't add any new transactions.

author: jih2nn
content: In the same vein, this is not guaranteed.

author: potuz
content: Then it's satisfied and the bit should be set

author: jih2nn
content: The builder didn't see that IL so they didn't set it. But when that IL doesn't add any new transaction, its payload will be IL-compliant.

author: potuz
content: That's fine, it's fine to drop this bid here anyway

author: soispoke
content: So it’s compliant by accident

author: soispoke
content: Idk why we would want that to work specifically

author: jih2nn
content: So you want something similar to PTC's negative vote? As in, what allows a proposer to reorg a payload honestly and safely.

author: potuz
content: This is attesters for the current block, they should reorg it on the spot if it chose the wrong bid

author: potuz
content: It protects the builder from eventually not seeing some lists

author: potuz
content: It lets the proposer to get the right bids over p2p

author: potuz
content: It doesn't make sense not to include it

author: potuz
content: And it definitely needs to be part of forkchoice

author: soispoke
content: High level it’s just an important part of the whole commit and reveal principle imo, you need to include the relevant information in what you commit to

author: soispoke
content: Otherwise it introduces unnecessary uncertainty

author: jih2nn
content: So the use case is a builder can be excused when they didn't see some IL that makes their payload not IL-compliant AND when they use trustless payment?

author: potuz
content: They aren't excused, they are simply not chosen

author: potuz
content: And the chain gets to see a valid Payload instead of none

author: jih2nn
content: They can be chosen but they won't be forced to pay.

author: potuz
content: With a vanilla implementation if they are chosen and attesters for any reason anyway make that block canonical, the builder pays

author: jih2nn
content: It looks similar to commit and reveal scheme but I don't think that's strictly true. You cannot derive the exact ILs that are committed. It's not 1:1 mapping.

author: potuz
content: But on the normal situation if both the builder and the proposer didn't see an includer even with the freeze time. Then the whole slot will be reorged

author: soispoke
content: Yeah I know it’s not 1 to 1 mapping but it’s additional/valuable info to improve liveness

author: jih2nn
content: I don't follow this one?

author: soispoke
content: I think this means builder and proposer didn’t see it but most attesters did

author: potuz
content: The only way an honest builder sends a non compliant bid is if it hasn't seen an includer. The only way an honest proposer chooses it is if it hasn't seen it either. If both of these things happen the block will be reorged by honest attesters

author: jih2nn
content: Right, I'm not in the best head space as a disclaimer. It's late in my timezone.

author: soispoke
content: We can definitely pick this conversation up later or on Monday just thought it was imprtant to flag it

author: jih2nn
content: Not sure if this is true. Assuming honest majority, the payload won't be reorged.

author: potuz
content: I think one point I want to stress is that even disregarding all the benefits I listed above. The biggest point is that it makes the incentives to be aligned. Without the bitlist and it being part of forkchoice, proposers rational behavior is to include censoring blocks if they pay more

author: potuz
content: And this alone should be enough of an argument

author: jih2nn
content: How can proposers canonicalize censoring payloads?

author: soispoke
content: They can’t but they can choose it

author: soispoke
content: And it will be reorged

author: soispoke
content: But it’s a liveness failure

author: potuz
content: They can canonicalize their blocks

author: jih2nn
content: They don't know whether the corresponding payload is IL-compliant or not. Their incentive is choosing the highest bid, but a payload will be reorged if it's not IL-compliant.

author: jih2nn
content: And this is why you guys want to have the bidlist and include it in the forkchoice IIUC

author: jih2nn
content: In this case, i.e., when the *highest* bidder didn't see some IL, it might also imply that other validators/builders are likely to not see it too. I'm just thinking about how severe this would be.

author: soispoke
content: That’s right, I think the most important thing is still to let the proposer chose bids that are aligned with his local view even if it’s not the view most other nodes have

author: potuz
content:
That's irrelevant here. Let's be more verbose. 

Without the bitlist in the bid. 

1) Censoring builder (even if honest but didn't see an includer) sends a bid that will not satisfy the proposer list, but pays more. 
2) Proposer chooses that bid, he doesn't care that the payload will be reorged, he will be included and the payload will pay him.

With the bitlist in the bid:

1) Same as above
2) Proposer will not choose that bid because if he does, the attesters will reorg his block and he wont be paid. 

That alone is enough of a reason. 

In addition you get: 

An honest but economically irrational proposer that wants to choose non-censoring payloads, may not even see non-censoring bids coming from P2P **because** p2p dropped them in favor of a censoring one. 

An honest but disconnected builder that does not want to censor, by chance got his payload chosen and now he's lost money.

author: jih2nn
content:
So this is not canonicalizing censoring payload as you said. It's just repeat of what's already discussed. Also, I want to point out that this is when using trustless payment.

> may not even see non-censoring bids coming from P2P because p2p dropped them in favor of a censoring one. 

Why is this happening?

author: jih2nn
content: Ah, wait. You said block.

author: soispoke
content: Beacon block

author: jih2nn
content: It's weird to say censoring "block". Payload is what can censor.

author: jih2nn
content: So this is the use case IIUC.

author: jih2nn
content: Let alone the impact of the use case.

author: jih2nn
content: > - _[IGNORE]_ this bid is the highest value bid seen for the tuple `(bid.slot, bid.parent_block_hash, bid.parent_block_root)`.

author: jih2nn
content: Ah, nvm. I misread it.

author: soispoke
content: not sure I’m following what you mean with the last messages but it was right that you can canonicalize a beacon block but not a payload

author: jih2nn
content: Thank you everyone for sharing your input. cc <@755590043632140352> <@725415098331430932> A use case of preventing an honest builder from being forced to pay when it shouldn't makes sense to me. I will think about the best way to implement this. For instance, there might be a better way than reorging a beacon block such as not forcing a poor builder a payment instead, so we can have a block at least.

author: potuz
content: The most important use case is to make it rational for the proposer not to include censoring Payloads

author: potuz
content: Otherwise it's irrational

author: jih2nn
content: What's the most important may vary depending on values or perspective IMO. Not saying one is not important or whatnot.

author: bharath.vedartham
content:
this might have already been covered but i m confused on why the builder can't just lie in the bitlist that they have satisfied the ILs by setting it to all 1's? The p2p gossip rules would pass and fork choice checks for the bitlist would work too? But then the payload would get reorged by next slot attesters. 
It just feels like a field which is very easy to manipulate? and attesters have no easy way to know whether the builder is lying or not?

author: potuz
content: The builder can always lie, malicious behavior can always bypass this

author: potuz
content: what is the problem with that?

author: bharath.vedartham
content:
yup, my question is does it make sense to have a fork choice rule which can quite easily be bypassed. I can imagine builders just setting the list to all 1's in the bid bypassing the p2p and forkchoice rules and then competing primarily on the bid value. 
I think having the bitlist field, integrating it into p2p and forkchoice is basically assuming that builders honestly set this bistlist.

author: potuz
content: the point is that builders are now honest participant in consensus, and they can have missed an includer. By not making this part of forkchoice, the proposer is incentivized to include this honest builder bid in its block and grieve an honest builder that just happen to have been disconnected and now is forced to pay

author: potuz
content: for a payload it didn't mean to construct
